speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 16, 1998-- Selma Engel, born Wynberg, is being interviewed in Branford, Connecticut, United States, in English by me, Betsy Stern. Today is October 16, 1998. Selma Engel, maiden name Wynberg is being interviewed by me, Betsy Stern, in Branford, Connecticut, United States, in English. Let's begin with your introducing yourself to us. Your name is--

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Selma Engel Wynberg. My maiden name is Wynberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you spell Wynberg for--

speaker: interviewee
text: W-Y-N-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born? I was born in Groningen, in northern Holland. I grow up, really, in Zwolle, Z-W-O-L-L-E. And what were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents had a small kosher hotel. And it was the only hotel in the state of Overijssel. It was really a province. In Holland, they call it a province. And that, it was on the cattle market. And in the cattle market business, were a lot of Jewish people involved. And they came down-- and it was kosher, of course. And then they came and slept, sometimes. It was a small hotel-- and came and eat there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your parents' names? My mother's name was Alida Wijnberg Nathans. No, her maiden name was Nathans. And my father was Samuel [NON-ENGLISH] Wijnberg. And when were you born, Selma?

speaker: interviewee
text: Long time ago. I was born in 1922, May the 15th, in Groningen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that makes you how old, today?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember Groningen at all, before you moved?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. I remember that my parents had a factory, a brush factory. And that the man brought me to the kindergarten, and I cried all the way. And I hated to go to school. That's-- I don't remember much, no-- very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: I forgot to ask you, did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have-- I had three brothers. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest was Bram, Marthyn, and Mauritz. And I was the youngest, from the four children. And my oldest brother was hidden in Holland. And he has four children. They live in Toronto. We are very close to his four children. He died when he was 60. He was overweight, and he had drank too much, and smoked too many cigarettes, I think, that was-- he was very unhappy when he went to Canada, really. He never made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's get back to the very early days. Do you remember the move to the new town, from Groningen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that we went-- my parents bought a hotel. And a Jewish hotel, for Jewish people, in Zwolle. And that I remember. But somehow, it was not a very-- It was very-- they had very difficult time. There was a redevelopment. And they had to-- after they bought the house, they found out there was a redevelopment. So they had to build another hotel. And yes, I remember there was-- and they made a tent, because the hotel was not finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had put up a very big tent. And when there was a storm, the tent went in the air. So the whole family went to the place, and they held the tent down, I think. That's what I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: But no, I don't remember much from the move. I don't know. I was seven years old. I should remember, but I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in the tent, when you first moved?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't live in the tent. We still lived in that old hotel that my parents bought. And then they build it-- were building a new hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go to school in Zwolle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I went to school in Zwolle.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to elementary school. And then I went-- when I was finished, I went to an [NON-ENGLISH] school. But I didn't want to-- I hated school. So my tough luck, they wanted to put me in high school. And I went to some kind of a high school, where you learn, also, like a trade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Were there many Jewish families in Zwolle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there were 600 Jews where I lived in Zwolle. I think something like that-- 600 Jews lived in Zwolle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have Jewish friends and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. We had-- I had Jewish friends. We had a lot of not-Jewish friends. And went to the  movie night. I remember it was kosher. And so I took my own sandwich with me. But I had more not-Jewish friends than Jewish friends. Yes, I remember that very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a synagogue. My mother was very orthodox, and we went every Shabbos morning to synagogue, and every Shabbos evening to the rabbi, what he tells us about the Torah. And we were very-- my mother was very active, and we were very religious at home, in Zwolle. Only, inside the house-- the religion was more inside the house. It was not that-- the not-Jewish people didn't know much about the Jews' religion, really, at all. Like here, in America, everybody knows about Yom Kippur and Rosh Hashanah. And in Holland, I don't think they know anything about it. But I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remember the holidays being celebrated at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, definitely, all the holidays-- Sukkot, and Simchas Torah. I remember my father and mother were-- [NON-ENGLISH] or something. I forgot how you call it. And then the whole qahal, the whole, the whole congregation came to our house. And they got pastries, and the [NON-ENGLISH] candies. Yeah, it was very-- it was-- every holiday was very-- we were very much-- and every day, every Friday was a holiday, in our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every Friday was a holiday.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, every Shabbos day after work, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother cook special?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Friday was the market in Holland, where there was-- at the hotel. And my parents made a living from the cattle market. All the Jewish people came to the hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Friday evening, then we had a big dinner. And we light the candles. But I don't remember, but I  think definitely she would light the candles, and [NON-ENGLISH], and things like that. And then she did a prayer after the dinner, and a prayer before the dinner. And we were always singing Jewish songs with the family-- special Jewish songs, which we sing now, the same songs, in the synagogue. And yeah, we did-- it was a very, because of my  mother, very religious. My brothers had to select the [NON-ENGLISH]. And we were-- and they wore arba kanfot. Tzitzit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with tzitzits on it. When they were teenagers, they didn't want to wear it anymore. But when they were young, yeah, we were an orthodox-- not with payos, and hats, and clothes. On the outside, we were not-- very assimilated. But in the house, we were very religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think that your family was more observant than other Jewish families in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we-- no, there were more, more Jewish families. I don't know. I was a teenager, and I didn't care, really. I was kosher. I didn't eat what was not allowed to eat. But father, I didn't-- was very orthodox, myself, because I thought it was very boring. We weren't allowed to go with a bicycle. We were not allowed to do anything, on Shabbat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so I was not so good, in to be very orthodox. I was kosher. And I did my prayers, perhaps, what I do. I remember, when I went to sleep I might have had a prayer, things like that. But not like my mother-- was very, very religious. And she was in-- and give it-- she was, how you call it? When somebody died, she helped. And only very orthodox women could do that. The burials?

speaker: interviewee
text: The burial.

speaker: interviewer
text: The burial society-- the Chevra.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she was Jewish, yeah. And she went every year. Every month she went to the [NON-ENGLISH.] And She looked, always so terrible, I thought. I didn't know what it really means, when I was young. So no, I-- what was the question?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the question was, did you think that your family was more observant than most?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, there were a lot of more observant people in Zwolle, I think. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a favorite holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: A favorite holiday? Yeah-- no, not really, no. We were so busy always. Like with Hanukkah, we didn't have Hanukkah. You  know, was busy. The hotel was busy. There was a Jewish, not-Jewish holiday. Sinterklaas is a big holiday for everybody. So there was-- no. No, I didn't have a favorite holiday. No, not really. Every holiday was really pleasant, to tell you the truth. We had a lot of people came to the house, and it was-- you felt good. It was nice, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wear special clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, got all these beautiful, nice clothes. And it was nice. There was a nice feeling in, in, in, it-- in the, in the qahal, in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Community.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the community. It was a nice feeling. It was nice. It was really-- you felt you belonged to something. It was, was very nice, yeah. I liked it, yeah, as a child too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel that you belonged in Holland, in your town, with your non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I felt very much Dutch, oh, yeah, very Dutch. And I felt very much for the queen, and-- oh, yeah. There was no other country even thinking about, ever to go to. And I, no-- I loved the hotel, while my parents were in that. And, no, was a very satisfying life. We went-- I belonged to a Zionist organization. And we went out with the bicycles. And we went to camps. And I went swimming in the rivers. And it was a wonderful life. No, I had a wonderful youth, and it was a wonderful life. Yes, no I never thought a minute to go away. When I was a little bit older, I wanted to go to Israel. When I saw it looked to be very exciting, I wanted to go to Israel. That, when I was-- but I wouldn't have gone, anyway. No, my parents would never have let me go. But I was very much in that group, because I was young and that's what I enjoyed going to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you or your parents talking, ever, about what was happening in Germany, before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they never did. And we had, many times, people would run away from Germany, stayed over in the hotel. And my parents have them a home. And also young men would run away from Germany. They stayed in the hotel. But somehow, they never-- because I didn't want to hear, I think. I just didn't want to hear it. I was more interested in boys and going out, than I want that seriousness. And I just-- no, I was a tomboy. No. I remember that they came to our house. But we never talked about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never knew why they were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never thought about it, I think. I was not a very serious girl. I was, I think I was just a tomboy. And I come from an unhappy marriage. So there was not much talking from my parents. So there wasn't much talking in the family, really, about things. So they know about it. And they know that there was something going on. And there came so many German Jews in Zwolle, where I come from. And they stayed there. And they were overpowering, almost.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they overpowering?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're very strong. And they were overpowering-- they were very-- most were very educated, very well-to-do, most of them. And they came to Zwolle. And they thought, always, that by then was everything better. We had a saying, [YIDDISH] everything better. That what the Germans-- they were very overpowering. But I remember as child, that way it was. Because there was always in hotel, there was always strangers there. So I don't remember there was much talking, as family, together, because they were always busy serving the guests in the hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm really brought up by the maids, because my mother was busy in the hotel, speaking with the-- and I was sometimes not allowed to come in the hotel, where the-- sitting with the salesman. All Jewish salesmen came over the hotel. So yeah, and I was already 17, 16, 17 years old. I was very pretty, I think, at that time. So they didn't let me go where the salesmen were there. You know, we were too old. I don't know. I really can't answer that question. I wonder sometimes myself. I was the youngest and the most spoiled one, I think. I really don't remember. Did your brothers talk about what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told you, we didn't-- I was the only, the youngest. They never took me serious. They never-- I was the only girl. I was the youngest. And I asked them sometimes things about boys or so. And they laughed. They never took me very serious, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things start to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1940-- first, my two brothers went in the army. And so there was-- when the Germans came into Holland-- and it was on a Friday morning, when it was a cattle market. And so there was a lot of Jewish salesmen were there. And they just thought it was the end of the world, already. It was the end already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they did even when they-- they start running home with the cars. And they drove the cars in the canals, on the ice. There was a very big-- that was a river. Because the Germans couldn't get the cars. And they were just-- it was turmoil. So they left the cars in the water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and it was just turmoil, because so many people-- they're all running home. And the only thing what they want was to get away. So that was on a Friday. And the war was over, five days later. I had one brother, was home with us. He was very sick. So he was home, with his wife. And my father wasn't in the picture. I think he was already sick and not home anymore, because I don't remember my father, at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing, those five days?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were sitting, listening to the radio with this brother and my mother. And I cleaned up. I'm a freaking cleaning. I'm still a freaking cleaning. So I cleaned up the hotel. It was a big mess from the cattle market, with all the dirt and things. So I cleaned up so that it was a little bit more cozy in the house. We didn't have really a living room. The hotel was divided, and the-- a room-- there was a big ballroom upstairs, and the same big room downstairs. And that was-- we divided that. That was a little corner, where we could sit by ourselves. So I put carpets on the floor, and I cleaned it up. And we were sitting, and we were just scared that what will happen, for the radio. We were sitting and listening to the radio.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear any guns, any planes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I hear planes. We hear planes, going over. But I didn't hear any shooting. And I didn't hear any guns. And, and-- no. And then five days later, the war was over. Yeah, my father-- it's very strange. I don't remember. My father was home, because he went looking for my brothers. And he found them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in the military, on the front somewhere. And he found them. And when he came back, he had a heart attack-- something like that. He died in 1941. And he was very sick. And so, it was a very big, confusing time. And then my brothers came back home. And I tell you, somehow that's all that I remember from that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the Germans coming to your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, they came. In Holland, they did it in a very special way-- not like in Poland and, perhaps, in other-- they let the Jews come, and they didn't do anything. The first year-- the first half-year, at least-- they didn't do anything with the Jews. And after a half year, they setting laws.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't have so much different, the first year. That only thing what was, that we were not allowed to go to school. That's what they did, I think, right away. But I read about it. But that I remember-- I remember-- that's what I remember. That was the first year, they didn't do too much. Because in 1942-- what happened? So they, they were-- the first year-- at the end of the first year, they made that we were not allowed to do anything. We were not allowed to go to school. That's the first thing what they did. And they made some Jewish schools. And we were not allowed to go out of the house, after six o'clock. I tell you, I don't remember exactly the time. I should have looked it up, but I didn't. That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't remember exactly the time-- if it was a half year, or a year. And then we weren't allowed to go out of the house. And we were not allowed to work, anymore. And then we were not allowed to go to school, what I say. And we were not allowed to entertain with non-Jewish people. They took away our radios. We were restricted, in everything, what we were not allowed to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they treat you? Were you afraid of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was happened, when my father died, so I was took over a lot from my father. And also, one brother was home. And he helped, too. And he had a girlfriend, so he went a lot to this girlfriend, too. And so I was a lot alone, in the hotel. And I remember that that was scary, because sometimes people came into the hotel. And you were scared to see not Jewish people. You couldn't say anything to them. We have to be-- and you didn't know who came in the hotel, in the evening, you know. And you didn't want to-- it was just-- it was just a very scary time for the-- I remember, there came once a man. And he was a no-good. And he wanted-- want to do something to me. And, and, and I was afraid, even to say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came-- we took another man that came. And he say he will help us, financially. No, he will help my brothers to get out of the country. And so, and he say, but he needs some money. And we give him-- mother, my mother and I were very impressed with that guy. And he talked us into give money.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we give him hundreds of dollars, when he will help my brothers. And my brothers were hidden somewhere, because they were not home-- but especially, one brother, the youngest brother, Marthyn. And, and-- but he didn't do anything. He just took the money for himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were scared. We were-- and my mother was scared. And I was scared. And it was-- and this man was just out to get things from us, you know. He, he, [NON-ENGLISH] us, tremendous. Is that the right word to say? He-- that's Dutch, I think. He-- we thought a great deal about him, but he was nothing, because he was just out to get money from us. But we didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, my mother-- when he talked, he looked very-- sounds very intelligent. And that really-- we thought that he would do something for us. But he didn't. And so, you know, and that, that I remember, things like that. And I was, of course, a young girl. And I was in love with him. I thought he was the greatest, because he was so nice to us. But he didn't do anything for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a very scary time, because it was an open hotel. And everybody could come in. And you couldn't close the doors, or things like that. It was a very scary time. But not the Germans-- the Germans, we didn't see the Germans, till one day somebody came what has a club-- a club for-- for men's club.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, the Germans took over that club. And he came to my mother and say, when you go out of the hotel-- when the head of the police told him that he could do that, then he will take-- well, we'll put it in another house. And he comes to the hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was going to take the hotel over?

speaker: interviewee
text: He would take the hotel for the club. So the head of the police from Zwolle came to my mother and said, do it. Because when the German takes it over, you're worse off. But it was the same thing. So after it was in 1940-- my father died in 1941, so he was buried from other hotel. So it was in end of 1941, beginning for 1942 .

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we-- that was the first time that we really have something to do with what the Germans did, you know. It came out from the-- when the man was with the Germans together, because he was sent out of that place. But he was-- he was-- so the Germans-- so we went out of that hotel.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my mother was only allowed to take with us what she could carry. And that was terrible, because, you know, there was that whole hotel belongs to her and everything in there. So they put us-- see, I don't remember the whole finesses, because I was the youngest from the family. And they never told me anything about financially part of anything. So, I don't know what exactly was happened. But I know we had to go out of the hotel. And there was somebody in Zwolle what had a very small house, was  a very poor little house in a lousy-- a terrible neighborhood. And we went in that hotel. And went in that little house then. But there was no bathroom. And it was very small. And it was terrible situation. There was no hot water, and there was no-- we had to sleep, all in one room. And there was a barrel there, and it smelled the whole house from. I thought it was horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was when-- and we were sitting in the evening. And then you hear the Germans with the boots on, with the iron on the-- walking around next to your door. And at that time, we had already backpacks. Everybody had a backpack staying by the door, that when they, when the Germans would all come in and call us away, that we would go away. And we had all the things what we wanted with us. Where did that idea come from, to have the backpacks?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think there was Jewish organization in Holland what helped their own Jews to go to Poland. And they, and they could save their lives. That's what I think it was. Because they helped organizing, that the Jews could go away organized well. We were a very organized country. And everything was organized. In the town hall, you could see what, with the religious in  back, what was. And so it was no problem for the Germans to find the Jews, and what was going on there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- the-- from who went out-- that I say that went a lot out from, from the, from the Germans, from the Jews, themselves. The Jews, themselves, were the ones who gave these directions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. That's what I think. And they told us to do this, and to do that. We got little papers in the house. And they told us to do this, and to do that. And we had these backpacks ready, because I think they told us what to do. Because how can we have another idea to do that?

speaker: interviewer
text: We've come to the end of this tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is she in the way?
